runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity for prime-age US workers conditional on employment, representing the percent change in hours for a 1 percent change in non-labor income.
citations: Imbens, Rubin, and Sacerdote (2001) lottery study
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the income effect on the intensive margin for prime-age workers are consistently negative but small. Lottery studies and experimental evidence generally point to an elasticity between -0.02 and -0.10, with zero often falling within the 95% interval for specific subgroups, indicating leisure is a normal good with low income sensitivity for those already attached to the labor force.

  "interpretation": "Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity for prime-age US workers conditional on employment, representing the percent change in hours for a 1 percent change in non-labor income.",

    "p25": -0.08,
    "p50": -0.05,
    "p75": -0.02,
    "p95": 0.0
  },
  "citations": [
    "Imbens, Rubin, and Sacerdote (2001) lottery study",
    "Chetty (2012) bounding study",
    "Blundell and MaCurdy (1999)"
  ],
  "reasoning_summary": "Empirical estimates of the income effect on the intensive margin for prime-age workers are consistently negative but small. Lottery studies and experimental evidence generally point to an elasticity between -0.02 and -0.10, with zero often falling within the 95% interval for specific subgroups, indicating leisure is a normal good with low income sensitivity for those already attached to the labor force."
}
